symbols.name: \(B\)
symbols.def: \(B\) is the number of matched blocks in a replicate.

symbols.name: \(b\)
symbols.def: \(b\) indexes a matched block.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal V\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal V=\bigsqcup_{b=1}^B\mathcal V_b\) is the base-member set.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal V_b\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal V_b=\{v\in\mathcal V:\beta(v)=b\}\) is block \(b\).

symbols.name: \(\beta\)
symbols.def: \(\beta(v)\) is the block containing \(v\).

symbols.name: \(v_{b,r}\)
symbols.def: For a pair block, \(v_{b,0}\) and \(v_{b,1}\) are its members, with \(r\in\{0,1\}\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal U\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal U=\mathcal V\times\{0,1\}\), where \((v,a)\) records member \(v\)'s potential-outcome arm.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal U_a\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal U_a=\mathcal V\times\{a\}\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal G\)
symbols.def: Each \(g\in\mathcal G\) induces a permutation \(\pi_g\) satisfying \(\beta(\pi_g(v))=\beta(v)\).

symbols.name: \(\pi_g\)
symbols.def: \(\pi_g\) is the within-block member permutation induced by \(g\).

symbols.name: \(g\cdot u\)
symbols.def: \(g\cdot(v,a)=(\pi_g(v),a)\).

symbols.name: \(S_2\)
symbols.def: \(S_2\) is the abstract identity-and-swap group. For a pair block \(b\), the notation \(s_b\) denotes its nonidentity swap and \(\langle s_b\rangle=\{e,s_b\}\cong S_2\) is invoked as a subgroup of \(\mathcal G\) only when \(\mathcal V_b=\{v_{b,0},v_{b,1}\}\) and \(\langle s_b\rangle\le\mathcal G\).

symbols.name: \(O_{b,a}\)
symbols.def: \(O_{b,a}=\{(v_{b,0},a),(v_{b,1},a)\}\) denotes an arm-specific two-state pair cell unconditionally. If \(\mathcal V_b=\{v_{b,0},v_{b,1}\}\) and the pure local swap subgroup \(\langle s_b\rangle\) is contained in \(\mathcal G\), then \(O_{b,a}\) is a global \(\mathcal G\)-orbit. Absent that sufficient audit, the notation alone makes no global-orbit claim; another subgroup, including a coupled-swap subgroup, may nevertheless generate the same orbit.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal O\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal O=\mathcal U/\mathcal G\) is the partition into arm-preserving member orbits.

symbols.name: \(K\)
symbols.def: \(K\) is a known outcome envelope.

symbols.name: \(X_{iv}\)
symbols.def: \(X_{iv}\) is member \(v\)'s baseline covariate in replicate \(i\).

symbols.name: \(Y_{iv}(a)\)
symbols.def: \(Y_{iv}(a)\) is member \(v\)'s potential outcome in arm \(a\) in replicate \(i\).

symbols.name: \(Y_v(a)\)
symbols.def: \(Y_v(a)\) denotes a potential-outcome coordinate in a generic latent array.

symbols.name: \(L_i\)
symbols.def: \(L_i=\{(X_{iv},Y_{iv}(0),Y_{iv}(1)):v\in\mathcal V\}\).

symbols.name: \(Z_i\)
symbols.def: \(Z_i=(Z_{iv}:v\in\mathcal V)\) is the realized matched-block assignment.

symbols.name: \(Y^{\mathrm{obs}}_{iv}\)
symbols.def: \(Y^{\mathrm{obs}}_{iv}=Y_{iv}(Z_{iv})\).

symbols.name: \(\mathsf O_i\)
symbols.def: \(\mathsf O_i=\{(X_{iv},Z_{iv},Y^{\mathrm{obs}}_{iv}):v\in\mathcal V\}\).

symbols.name: \(D_i\)
symbols.def: \(D_i=(L_i,Z_i)\) is used only in probability statements and proofs.

symbols.name: \(g\mathsf O_i\)
symbols.def: \((g\mathsf O_i)_{\pi_g(v)}=(X_{iv},Z_{iv},Y^{\mathrm{obs}}_{iv})\).

symbols.name: \(P\)
symbols.def: \(P\) is the superpopulation law of a generic latent matched-block array.

symbols.name: \(\nu\)
symbols.def: \(\nu\) is the known assignment law.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal T\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal T\) is external observed training data.

symbols.name: \(\widehat m_a\)
symbols.def: \(\widehat m_a\) is an arbitrary measurable map trained only from \(\mathcal T\).

symbols.name: \(q_{\mathrm{tar}}\)
symbols.def: \(q_{\mathrm{tar}}\) is fixed before scored data are observed.

symbols.name: \(T\)
symbols.def: \(T\sim q_{\mathrm{tar}}\) is the one common target member.

symbols.name: \(T^a\)
symbols.def: \(T^a=\iota_a(T)\).

symbols.name: \(X_{0T}\)
symbols.def: \(X_{0T}\) is observed after target selection.

symbols.name: \(Y_{0T}(a)\)
symbols.def: \(Y_{0T}(a)\) is proof-only except at the realized arm.

symbols.name: \(q_{\mathrm{tar},a}\)
symbols.def: \(q_{\mathrm{tar},a}(\iota_a(v))=q_{\mathrm{tar}}(v)\), with zero mass outside \(\mathcal U_a\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal H\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal H\) indexes allowed observable selector kernels.

symbols.name: \(\kappa_{h,a}\)
symbols.def: \(\kappa_{h,a}\) selects an observed arm-\(a\) member under assignment \(z\).

symbols.name: \(q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{h,a}\)
symbols.def: \(q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{h,a}(v,a)=\sum_z\nu(z)\kappa_{h,a}(v\mid z)\), with zero mass outside \(\mathcal U_a\).

symbols.name: \(w\)
symbols.def: When \(\mathcal H\) is finite and nonempty, \(w=(w_h:h\in\mathcal H)\) is one fixed or prespecified mixture shared by both arms. No such \(w\) exists when \(\mathcal H=\varnothing\).

symbols.name: \(\kappa_{w,a}\)
symbols.def: \(\kappa_{w,a}\) is the implemented mixture kernel.

symbols.name: \(q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{w,a}\)
symbols.def: \(q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{w,a}=\sum_{h\in\mathcal H}w_hq^{\mathrm{obs}}_{h,a}\).

symbols.name: \(J_{i,a}\)
symbols.def: \(J_{i,a}\sim\kappa_{w,a}(\cdot\mid Z_i)\).

symbols.name: \(S_{i,a}\)
symbols.def: \(S_{i,a}=|Y^{\mathrm{obs}}_{iJ_{i,a}}-\widehat m_a(X_{iJ_{i,a}})|\).

symbols.name: \(A\)
symbols.def: \(A_{(a,O),h}=\sum_{u\in O}q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{h,a}(u)\).

symbols.name: \(b_{\mathrm{LP}}\)
symbols.def: \((b_{\mathrm{LP}})_{a,O}=\sum_{u\in O}q_{\mathrm{tar},a}(u)\).

symbols.name: \(\delta_{a,O}(w)\)
symbols.def: \(\delta_{a,O}(w)=q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{w,a}(O)-q_{\mathrm{tar},a}(O)\).

symbols.name: \(\Delta_a(w)\)
symbols.def: \(\Delta_a(w)=\tfrac12\sum_{O\in\mathcal O}|\delta_{a,O}(w)|\).

symbols.name: \(M\)
symbols.def: \(M\) is the number of calibration replicates.

symbols.name: \(\alpha\)
symbols.def: \(\alpha\) is the effect miscoverage level.

symbols.name: \(k\)
symbols.def: \(k=\lceil(M+1)(1-\alpha/2)\rceil\).

symbols.name: \(r_{a,(k)}\)
symbols.def: \(r_{a,(k)}\) is the \(k\)-th order statistic of the \(M\) calibration scores when \(k\le M\), and \(+\infty\) when \(k=M+1\).

symbols.name: \(I_{a,w}\)
symbols.def: \(I_{a,w}=[\widehat m_a(X_{0T})-r_{a,(k)},\widehat m_a(X_{0T})+r_{a,(k)}]\cap[-K,K]\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal C_w\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal C_w=I_{1,w}-I_{0,w}\), where both arm intervals are clipped to \([-K,K]\).

symbols.name: \(p_{a,O}\)
symbols.def: \(p_{a,O}=q_{\mathrm{tar},a}(O)\).

symbols.name: \(q_{a,O}\)
symbols.def: \(q_{a,O}=q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{w,a}(O)\).

symbols.name: \(\lambda_{a,O}\)
symbols.def: Under orbit overlap, \(\lambda_{a,O}=p_{a,O}/q_{a,O}\) when \(q_{a,O}>0\), and \(\lambda_{a,O}=0\) when \(p_{a,O}=q_{a,O}=0\).

symbols.name: \(O_{i,a}\)
symbols.def: \(O_{i,a}\) is the orbit containing \(U_{i,a}\).

symbols.name: \(O_{T,a}\)
symbols.def: \(O_{T,a}\) is the orbit containing \(T^a\).

symbols.name: \(r^{\mathrm W}_{a,w}\)
symbols.def: \(r^{\mathrm W}_{a,w}\) is the arm-\(a\) orbit-weighted left-continuous \((1-\varepsilon_a)\)-quantile in \(\texttt{def:weighted-rank-inversion}\).

symbols.name: \(I^{\mathrm W}_{a,w}\)
symbols.def: \(I^{\mathrm W}_{a,w}=[\widehat m_a(X_{0T})-r^{\mathrm W}_{a,w},\widehat m_a(X_{0T})+r^{\mathrm W}_{a,w}]\cap[-K,K]\), where \(r^{\mathrm W}_{a,w}\) uses level \(\varepsilon_a\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal C^{\mathrm W}_w\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal C^{\mathrm W}_w=I^{\mathrm W}_{1,w}-I^{\mathrm W}_{0,w}\), with armwise levels \(\varepsilon_1\) and \(\varepsilon_0\), respectively.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal P_{\mathcal G,K}\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal P_{\mathcal G,K}\) is defined by \(\texttt{def:invariant-laws}\).

symbols.name: \(Q_k\)
symbols.def: \(Q_k(x_1,\ldots,x_M)\) is the \(k\)-th order statistic if \(k\le M\), and \(+\infty\) if \(k=M+1\).

symbols.name: \(s_a\)
symbols.def: \(s_a\) assigns an orbit-constant score in arm \(a\).

symbols.name: \(\Gamma_{M,\alpha}(w)\)
symbols.def: \(\Gamma_{M,\alpha}(w)\) is the equal-split exact finite worst joint-armwise-inclusion certificate deficit defined by \(\texttt{def:coverage-frontier}\); it is not the actual effect-set coverage deficit.

symbols.name: \(\rho_{M,\alpha}\)
symbols.def: \(\rho_{M,\alpha}=(M+1-k)/(M+1)=\lfloor(M+1)\alpha/2\rfloor/(M+1)\) is the exact one-arm finite-rank error.

symbols.name: \(\boldsymbol\varepsilon\)
symbols.def: \(\boldsymbol\varepsilon=(\varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1)\) allocates the effect miscoverage budget across the two armwise procedures.

symbols.name: \(k_a\)
symbols.def: \(k_a=\lceil(M+1)(1-\varepsilon_a)\rceil\).

symbols.name: \(\rho_{M,\varepsilon_a}\)
symbols.def: \(\rho_{M,\varepsilon_a}=(M+1-k_a)/(M+1)=\lfloor(M+1)\varepsilon_a\rfloor/(M+1)\).

symbols.name: \(Q_{k_a}\)
symbols.def: \(Q_{k_a}\) is the \(k_a\)-th order statistic when \(k_a\le M\), and \(+\infty\) when \(k_a=M+1\).

symbols.name: \(r_{a,(k_a)}\)
symbols.def: \(r_{a,(k_a)}\) is the \(k_a\)-th arm-\(a\) calibration-score order statistic when \(k_a\le M\), and \(+\infty\) otherwise.

symbols.name: \(I^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_{a,w}\)
symbols.def: \(I^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_{a,w}=[\widehat m_a(X_{0T})-r_{a,(k_a)},\widehat m_a(X_{0T})+r_{a,(k_a)}]\cap[-K,K]\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal C^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_w\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal C^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_w=I^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_{1,w}-I^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_{0,w}\).

symbols.name: \(\Gamma_{M,\alpha,\boldsymbol\varepsilon}(w)\)
symbols.def: \(\Gamma_{M,\alpha,\boldsymbol\varepsilon}(w)\) is the exact finite worst joint-armwise-inclusion certificate deficit over bounded orbit-constant scores defined by \(\texttt{def:coverage-frontier}\); it is not the actual effect-set coverage deficit.

symbols.name: \(D_*\)
symbols.def: When \(\mathcal H\) is finite and nonempty, \(D_*\) is the minimum total two-arm orbit imbalance in \(\texttt{def:minimum-imbalance-common-selector}\); it is undefined for the empty library.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal W_*\)
symbols.def: When \(\mathcal H\) is finite and nonempty, \(\mathcal W_*\) is the nonempty set of mixtures attaining \(D_*\); it is undefined for the empty library.

symbols.name: \(w_*\)
symbols.def: When \(\mathcal H\) is finite and nonempty, \(w_*\) is any prespecified minimum-imbalance common selector in \(\mathcal W_*\); it is undefined for the empty library.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal A_{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal A_{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}=\{a\in\{0,1\}:k_a\le M\}\) is the set of arms whose rank threshold is finite under the prespecified error allocation.

symbols.name: \(D_{*,\mathcal A}\)
symbols.def: When \(\mathcal H\) is finite and nonempty, \(D_{*,\mathcal A}\) is the minimum orbit imbalance over rows belonging to \(\mathcal A_{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}\) in \(\texttt{def:minimum-imbalance-common-selector}\); it is undefined when \(\mathcal H=\varnothing\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal W_{*,\mathcal A}\)
symbols.def: When \(\mathcal H\) is finite and nonempty, \(\mathcal W_{*,\mathcal A}\) is the nonempty set of mixtures attaining \(D_{*,\mathcal A}\); if \(\mathcal A_{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}=\varnothing\), it equals \(\Delta(\mathcal H)\). It is undefined when \(\mathcal H=\varnothing\).

symbols.name: \(w_{*,\mathcal A}\)
symbols.def: When \(\mathcal H\) is finite and nonempty, \(w_{*,\mathcal A}\) is any deterministically tie-broken, prespecified member of \(\mathcal W_{*,\mathcal A}\); it is undefined when \(\mathcal H=\varnothing\).

novel.justification: A prespecified bounded clinical, educational, or proportion scale is realistic and permits auditable finite orbit-separation and zero-coverage constructions.

This proposal-specific technical superpopulation condition imposes iid matched-block experiments to obtain split-conformal exchangeability; it is not an assumption or theorem of Wang, Li, and Yu's Statistica Sinica preprint.
External observed training is an implementable split construction and permits exact statements conditional on the trained predictor maps.
A target member fixed independently of scored data is a transparent deployment rule for an externally selected facility and preserves one coherent target across both arms.

assumptions.condition: \(\kappa_{h,a}(v\mid z)\ge0,\quad\sum_{v\in\mathcal V}\kappa_{h,a}(v\mid z)=1,\quad z_v\ne a\Rightarrow\kappa_{h,a}(v\mid z)=0\quad\forall h\in\mathcal H,\ a\in\{0,1\},\ v\in\mathcal V,\ z\text{ with }\nu(z)>0\)

novel.justification: These kernels select only a member whose realized arm is observed on every assignment that can occur under the known randomization law.

assumptions.condition: \(\mathcal L((J_{i,a})_{1\le i\le M,\ a\in\{0,1\}}\mid(L_i)_{0\le i\le M},(Z_i)_{0\le i\le M},T,\mathcal T)=\prod_{i=1}^M\prod_{a=0}^1\kappa_{w,a}(\cdot\mid Z_i)\)

novel.justification: Independent auxiliary draws from observable kernels are implementable and prevent calibration selection from using latent arrays or the target.

definitions.name: local pair-swap specialization
definitions.construction: For named pair members define \(O_{b,a}=\{(v_{b,0},a),(v_{b,1},a)\}\) as a two-state pair cell, without thereby asserting that it is a global orbit. If \(\mathcal V_b=\{v_{b,0},v_{b,1}\}\) and the pure local swap subgroup \(\langle s_b\rangle=\{e,s_b\}\) is contained in \(\mathcal G\), then \(s_b(v_{b,r},a)=(v_{b,1-r},a)\) for \(r,a\in\{0,1\}\), \(s_b\) fixes members outside block \(b\), and it relabels complete records by \(\texttt{def:group-action}\). Because every group element preserves blocks and arms and block \(b\) has exactly two members, this sufficient audit implies that \(O_{b,0}\) and \(O_{b,1}\) are global \(\mathcal G\)-orbits. The audit is not necessary: a coupled-swap subgroup may also generate the same orbit. Absent the audit, the pair-cell notation alone makes no global-orbit claim. Pair-specific propositions and examples are invoked after this sufficient audit; a product specialization such as \(S_2^B\) verifies every required pure local swap separately.

definitions.name: observable replicated matched-randomization experiment
definitions.construction: For each \(i\in\{0,\ldots,M\}\), draw \(L_i\), draw \(Z_i\sim\nu\), set \(Y^{\mathrm{obs}}_{iv}=Y_{iv}(Z_{iv})\), and form \(\mathsf O_i=\{(X_{iv},Z_{iv},Y^{\mathrm{obs}}_{iv}):v\in\mathcal V\}\). Draw the one common \(T\sim q_{\mathrm{tar}}\), observe \(X_{0T}\) after selection, and use neither \(Y_{0T}(0)\) nor \(Y_{0T}(1)\) in fitting or calibration.

definitions.name: \(\mathcal P_{\mathcal G,K}\)
definitions.construction: \(\mathcal P_{\mathcal G,K}=\{P:P\circ(gL_i)^{-1}=P\quad\forall g\in\mathcal G,\quad\max_{v\in\mathcal V,\ a\in\{0,1\}}|Y_v(a)|\le K\}\).

definitions.name: observable common selector
definitions.construction: For every \(i\in\{1,\ldots,M\}\) and \(a\in\{0,1\}\), set \(\kappa_{w,a}=\sum_{h\in\mathcal H}w_h\kappa_{h,a}\), draw \(J_{i,a}\sim\kappa_{w,a}(\cdot\mid Z_i)\), set \(U_{i,a}=(J_{i,a},a)\), and set \(q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{w,a}=\sum_{h\in\mathcal H}w_hq^{\mathrm{obs}}_{h,a}\).

definitions.name: unweighted observable rank inversion
definitions.construction: Prespecify \(\boldsymbol\varepsilon=(\varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1)\) with \(\varepsilon_a>0\) and \(\varepsilon_0+\varepsilon_1\le\alpha\), and set \(k_a=\lceil(M+1)(1-\varepsilon_a)\rceil\). For each \(a\in\{0,1\}\), compute \(S_{i,a}=|Y^{\mathrm{obs}}_{iJ_{i,a}}-\widehat m_a(X_{iJ_{i,a}})|\), take \(r_{a,(k_a)}\), set \(I^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_{a,w}=[\widehat m_a(X_{0T})-r_{a,(k_a)},\widehat m_a(X_{0T})+r_{a,(k_a)}]\cap[-K,K]\), and return \(\mathcal C^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_w=I^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_{1,w}-I^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_{0,w}\). If \(k_a=M+1\), set \(r_{a,(k_a)}=+\infty\), so \(I^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_{a,w}=[-K,K]\). For proof ranks only, append independent continuous uniforms and use lexicographic ranks; reported closed intervals use weak inclusion. Under \(\varepsilon_0=\varepsilon_1=\alpha/2\), define \(k=k_0=k_1\), \(r_{a,(k)}=r_{a,(k_a)}\), \(I_{a,w}=I^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_{a,w}\), and \(\mathcal C_w=\mathcal C^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_w\), recovering the symmetric construction.

definitions.name: randomization-integrated orbit-weighted rank inversion
definitions.construction: Prespecify \(\boldsymbol\varepsilon=(\varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1)\) with \(\varepsilon_a>0\) and \(\varepsilon_0+\varepsilon_1\le\alpha\). For each arm \(a\), place mass \(\lambda_{a,O_{i,a}}\) at each \(S_{i,a}\), place mass \(\lambda_{a,O_{T,a}}\) at \(+\infty\), normalize the total mass, and take its left-continuous \((1-\varepsilon_a)\)-quantile \(r^{\mathrm W}_{a,w}\). Set \(I^{\mathrm W}_{a,w}=[\widehat m_a(X_{0T})-r^{\mathrm W}_{a,w},\widehat m_a(X_{0T})+r^{\mathrm W}_{a,w}]\cap[-K,K]\), and return \(\mathcal C^{\mathrm W}_w=I^{\mathrm W}_{1,w}-I^{\mathrm W}_{0,w}\). Under \(\varepsilon_0=\varepsilon_1=\alpha/2\), this is the previous symmetric weighted construction. Thus the declared estimand functional \(\mathcal C^{\mathrm W}_w\) now denotes the arm-specific weighted procedure while retaining its equal-split specialization.

definitions.name: bounded orbit-separating construction
definitions.construction: For \(a\in\{0,1\}\) and \(O\in\mathcal O\), set \(\widehat m_0=\widehat m_1=0\), set \(Y_v(a)=K\mathbf 1\{\iota_a(v)\in O\}\), set \(Y_v(1-a)=0\), and randomize the orbit-constant prototype by a uniform \(g\in\mathcal G\) before drawing \(Z_i\sim\nu\).

definitions.name: bounded disjoint-orbit construction
definitions.construction: Fix \(a\in\{0,1\}\). Set \(\widehat m_0=\widehat m_1=0\), set \(Y_v(a)=K\) on target-supported arm-\(a\) orbits, set \(Y_v(a)=0\) on every state with positive calibration mass and on remaining states, set \(Y_v(1-a)=0\), and randomize the orbit-constant prototype by a uniform \(g\in\mathcal G\) before drawing \(Z_i\sim\nu\).

definitions.name: finite common-target orbit-constant joint-inclusion certificate frontier
definitions.construction:
For prespecified \(\boldsymbol\varepsilon=(\varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1)\), define
\[
\Gamma_{M,\alpha,\boldsymbol\varepsilon}(w)=\max_{\substack{s_a:\mathcal O\to[0,K]\\a\in\{0,1\}}}\left[(1-\alpha)-\Pr\left\{s_1(O_{T,1})\le Q_{k_1}(s_1(O_{1,1}),\ldots,s_1(O_{M,1}))\ \text{and}\ s_0(O_{T,0})\le Q_{k_0}(s_0(O_{1,0}),\ldots,s_0(O_{M,0}))\right\}\right]_+.
\]
Here \(O_{i,a}\sim q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{w,a}\), the target orbits are the two lifts of the one common \(T\sim q_{\mathrm{tar}}\), and the calibration draws use \(\texttt{ass:selector-auxiliary-randomization}\), including their within-replicate cross-arm coupling. The functional is restricted to bounded orbit-constant score maps. Its probability object is the joint armwise rank-inclusion event, so \(\Gamma_{M,\alpha,\boldsymbol\varepsilon}(w)\) is the exact worst joint-armwise-inclusion certificate deficit over that score class, not the actual effect-set coverage deficit. Under \(\varepsilon_0=\varepsilon_1=\alpha/2\), set \(\Gamma_{M,\alpha}(w)=\Gamma_{M,\alpha,\boldsymbol\varepsilon}(w)\), recovering the symmetric joint-inclusion certificate frontier.

definitions.name: exact finite-rank error
definitions.construction:
For \(N=M+1\), prespecified \(\boldsymbol\varepsilon=(\varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1)\), and \(k_a=\lceil N(1-\varepsilon_a)\rceil\), define
\[
\rho_{M,\varepsilon_a}=\frac{N-k_a}{N}=\frac{\lfloor N\varepsilon_a\rfloor}{N},\qquad a\in\{0,1\}.
\]
Under \(\varepsilon_0=\varepsilon_1=\alpha/2\), write their common value as
\[
\rho_{M,\alpha}=\frac{M+1-k}{M+1}=\frac{\lfloor(M+1)\alpha/2\rfloor}{M+1}.
\]

definitions.name: minimum-imbalance common selector
definitions.construction:
For every finite selector library define \(\Delta(\mathcal H)=\{w\in\mathbb R^{|\mathcal H|}:w_h\ge0\ \forall h,\ \sum_{h\in\mathcal H}w_h=1\}\); in particular \(\Delta(\varnothing)=\varnothing\). If \(\mathcal H=\varnothing\), no selector mixture, implemented kernel, selector-indexed ordinary or weighted procedure, \(D_*\), \(D_{*,\mathcal A}\), \(\mathcal W_*\), \(\mathcal W_{*,\mathcal A}\), \(w_*\), or \(w_{*,\mathcal A}\) exists. If \(\mathcal H\) is finite and nonempty, retain the joint two-arm diagnostics
\[
D_*=\min_{w\in\Delta(\mathcal H)}\frac12\sum_{a=0}^1\sum_{O\in\mathcal O}|(Aw-b_{\mathrm{LP}})_{a,O}|,\qquad
\mathcal W_*=\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{w\in\Delta(\mathcal H)}\frac12\sum_{a=0}^1\sum_{O\in\mathcal O}|(Aw-b_{\mathrm{LP}})_{a,O}|.
\]
Their absolute-value linear program minimizes \(\frac12\sum_{a=0}^1\sum_Ot_{a,O}\) subject to \(-t_{a,O}\le(Aw-b_{\mathrm{LP}})_{a,O}\le t_{a,O}\), \(t_{a,O}\ge0\), \(w\in\Delta(\mathcal H)\), and their dual is
\[
\max_{\lVert y\rVert_\infty\le1/2}\left\{\min_{h\in\mathcal H}y^\top A_{\cdot h}-y^\top b_{\mathrm{LP}}\right\}.
\]
For the prespecified armwise ranks define \(\mathcal A_{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}=\{a\in\{0,1\}:k_a\le M\}\) and
\[
D_{*,\mathcal A}=\min_{w\in\Delta(\mathcal H)}\frac12\sum_{a\in\mathcal A_{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}}\sum_{O\in\mathcal O}|(Aw-b_{\mathrm{LP}})_{a,O}|,\qquad
\mathcal W_{*,\mathcal A}=\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{w\in\Delta(\mathcal H)}\frac12\sum_{a\in\mathcal A_{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}}\sum_{O\in\mathcal O}|(Aw-b_{\mathrm{LP}})_{a,O}|.
\]
The active-row linear program minimizes \(\frac12\sum_{a\in\mathcal A_{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}}\sum_Ot_{a,O}\) subject to the same two-sided constraints only for \(a\in\mathcal A_{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}\), together with \(t_{a,O}\ge0\) and \(w\in\Delta(\mathcal H)\). Its dual is
\[
\max_{\substack{\lVert y\rVert_\infty\le1/2\\y_{a,O}=0\ \mathrm{for}\ a\notin\mathcal A_{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}}}\left\{\min_{h\in\mathcal H}y^\top A_{\cdot h}-y^\top b_{\mathrm{LP}}\right\}.
\]
If \(\mathcal A_{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}=\varnothing\), define \(D_{*,\mathcal A}=0\) and \(\mathcal W_{*,\mathcal A}=\Delta(\mathcal H)\), and choose \(w_{*,\mathcal A}\) without solving an LP. The full objects \(D_*\) and \(\mathcal W_*\) remain the joint two-arm balance diagnostics; \(D_{*,\mathcal A}=0\) characterizes only active-row balance unless both arms are active. Primal and dual equality, attainment, and the coverage certificates are proved in \(\texttt{thm:active-arm-minimum-imbalance-selector-certificate}\).

definitions.name: simplex vertex, edge, face, and rank-one exact-key prepass
definitions.construction:
For a finite set \(\Pi_\star\) of probability mass functions on \(\{0,1\}^2\), first set
\[
\Pi_{\mathrm{vert}}=\{\pi\in\Pi_\star:|\operatorname{supp}(\pi)|=1\},
\qquad
\Pi_{\mathrm{edge}}=\{\pi\in\Pi_\star\setminus\Pi_{\mathrm{vert}}:|\operatorname{supp}(\pi)|=2\}.
\]
For \(\pi\in\Pi_{\mathrm{vert}}\), let \(u=(x,y)\) be its unique support point and return \(\mathbf 1\{Mx<k_0,\ My<k_1\}\). For \(\pi\in\Pi_{\mathrm{edge}}\), order its two support points \(u=(u_0,u_1)\) and \(v=(v_0,v_1)\) deterministically; both possible orientations may be compared, with the smaller certified charge chosen and lexicographic tie-breaking. Put \(p=\pi_v\in(0,1)\), let \(N\sim\operatorname{Bin}(M,p)\), and define \(c_a=Mu_a\) and \(d_a=v_a-u_a\) for \(a\in\{0,1\}\). Set
\[
L_\pi=\max\!\left(\{0\}\cup\{c_a-k_a+1:d_a=-1\}\right),\qquad
U_\pi=\min\!\left(\{M\}\cup\{k_a-c_a-1:d_a=1\}\right).
\]
If \(d_a=0\) and \(c_a\ge k_a\) for some \(a\), declare \([L_\pi,U_\pi]\) empty. Otherwise return
\[
D_{M,k_0,k_1}(\pi)=\Pr\{L_\pi\le N\le U_\pi\}.
\]
For a nonempty interval define
\[
\eta_\pi=\min\!\left\{
\min\{L_\pi,M-L_\pi\}+U_\pi-L_\pi+1,\ 
L_\pi+M-U_\pi,\ 
\min\{U_\pi+1,M-U_\pi\}+\min\{L_\pi,M-L_\pi+1\}
\right\},
\]
and set \(\eta_\pi=0\) for an empty interval. Direct interval summation, complementary left-and-right tails, and a difference of two shorter binomial tails are three exact alternatives; choose the least charge deterministically and write \(\mathsf E_M(\pi)=1+\lceil\log_2 M\rceil+\eta_\pi\). The edge evaluation uses \(O(\mathsf E_M(\pi))\) exact arithmetic operations and \(O(1)\) auxiliary workspace.

For every remaining support-three key and every corner orientation \(\sigma\in\{L,U\}^2\), let \(\pi^\sigma\) be the transformed four-cell law and let \(t_a^\sigma=k_a\) under \(L\) and \(t_a^\sigma=M-k_a+1\) under \(U\). Define
\[
\Sigma_{\mathrm{face}}(\pi)=
\{\sigma:\pi^\sigma_{11}=0,\ t_0^\sigma+t_1^\sigma>M\},
\qquad
\Pi_{\mathrm{face}}=
\{\pi\in\Pi_\star\setminus(\Pi_{\mathrm{vert}}\cup\Pi_{\mathrm{edge}}):
\Sigma_{\mathrm{face}}(\pi)\ne\varnothing\}.
\]
For \(\sigma\in\Sigma_{\mathrm{face}}(\pi)\), let \(T_a^\sigma\) be the transformed terminal count and set
\[
J_\sigma=
1-\Pr\{T_0^\sigma\ge t_0^\sigma\}
 -\Pr\{T_1^\sigma\ge t_1^\sigma\}.
\]
Reconstruct \(D_{M,k_0,k_1}(\pi)\) from \(J_\sigma\) by the \(LL\), \(LU\), \(UL\), or \(UU\) corner identity. Each required reconstruction marginal is the complement of one of the two tails already evaluated. With
\[
\ell_M(t)=1+\lceil\log_2 M\rceil+\min\{t,M-t+1\},
\qquad
\mathsf F_M(\pi)=1+
\min_{\sigma\in\Sigma_{\mathrm{face}}(\pi)}
\{\ell_M(t_0^\sigma)+\ell_M(t_1^\sigma)\},
\]
choose a minimizing orientation and break ties lexicographically. The face evaluation uses \(O(\mathsf F_M(\pi))\) exact arithmetic operations and \(O(1)\) auxiliary workspace.

For every remaining support-four key, test the exact memoized-cell identity
\[
\pi_{00}\pi_{11}=\pi_{01}\pi_{10}.
\]
Define
\[
\Pi_{\mathrm{rank1}}=
\{\pi\in\Pi_\star\setminus
(\Pi_{\mathrm{vert}}\cup\Pi_{\mathrm{edge}}\cup\Pi_{\mathrm{face}}):
|\operatorname{supp}(\pi)|=4,\ 
\pi_{00}\pi_{11}=\pi_{01}\pi_{10}\}.
\]
For \(\pi\in\Pi_{\mathrm{rank1}}\), put
\[
\theta_0=\pi_{10}+\pi_{11},\qquad
\theta_1=\pi_{01}+\pi_{11},\qquad
\bar k_a=\min\{k_a,M-k_a+1\},
\]
and evaluate
\[
D_{M,k_0,k_1}(\pi)=
H_{M,k_0}(\theta_0)H_{M,k_1}(\theta_1),
\qquad
H_{M,k}(\theta)=\Pr\{\operatorname{Bin}(M,\theta)<k\}.
\]
With
\[
\mathsf R_{M,k_0,k_1}
=1+2\lceil\log_2M\rceil+\bar k_0+\bar k_1,
\]
the rank-one evaluation uses \(O(\mathsf R_{M,k_0,k_1})\) exact arithmetic operations and \(O(1)\) auxiliary workspace, with deterministic exact testing and no approximation. Only keys in
\(\Pi_\star\setminus(\Pi_{\mathrm{vert}}\cup\Pi_{\mathrm{edge}}\cup\Pi_{\mathrm{face}}\cup\Pi_{\mathrm{rank1}})\)
enter the remaining rolling, multinomial-sum, or positive-corner dispatcher.

statements.statement: Conditional on \(\mathcal T\), joint orbit balance is equivalent to equality in law of \(S_{i,a}\) and \(S_{0,a}\), for each \(a\in\{0,1\}\), uniformly over \(P\in\mathcal P_{\mathcal G,K}\) and arbitrary fixed measurable trained predictor maps \(\widehat m_a:\mathsf X\to\mathbb R\). Under balance, conditional on \(\mathcal T\), \(S_{1,a},\ldots,S_{M,a},S_{0,a}\) are iid for each arm. The assertion is armwise and makes no joint-exchangeability claim across arms. If an orbit mass differs, \(\texttt{def:orbit-separating-law}\) gives a bounded invariant law and fixed predictor maps with unequal score laws.
statements.justification: The theorem gives a universal finite-orbit mass characterization for equality of assignment-selected calibration and deployment score laws over iid replicated invariant arrays, together with an orbit-separating necessity witness. Hemerik--Goeman and Ramdas et al. are finite permutation/group-testing comparators \cite{HemerikGoeman2018,RamdasBarberCandesTibshirani2023}, Dobriban--Yu is a group-symmetry prediction comparator \cite{DobribanYu2025}, and Lei--Candès and Wang--Li--Yu are causal/design comparators \cite{LeiCandes2021,WangLiYu2025}; no priority or absence claim is made.
statements.gap: The comparison concerns different targets and mechanisms: testing under group transformations or predictive validity under symmetry does not itself impose equality of the calibration-selector and deployment-target orbit mixtures. The proof uses normalized realized-arm selector kernels and conditional selector draws, not selector equivariance.
statements.consumer: Use orbit-mass equality as the exact armwise audit for universal score-law equality in the replicated-array model; if a mass differs, use the bounded orbit-separating witness.

statements.statement: Adopt \(\Delta(\varnothing)=\varnothing\) and \(\operatorname{conv}(\varnothing)=\varnothing\). There exists \(w\in\Delta(\mathcal H)\) jointly orbit-balancing both arms if and only if \(b_{\mathrm{LP}}\in\operatorname{conv}\{A_{\cdot h}:h\in\mathcal H\}\). Equivalently, the stacked LP in \(\texttt{def:common-selector-feasibility-lp}\) is feasible. If \(\mathcal H=\varnothing\), infeasibility is certified by \(\sum_{h\in\varnothing}w_h=0\ne1\); no selector mixture, implemented kernel, selector-indexed ordinary or weighted procedure, \(D_*\), \(\mathcal W_*\), or \(w_*\) exists. If \(\mathcal H\ne\varnothing\) and the condition fails, a strict Farkas hyperplane separates \(b_{\mathrm{LP}}\) from the nonempty convex hull. Fix a prespecified \(\boldsymbol\varepsilon=(\varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1)\) with \(\varepsilon_a>0\) and \(\varepsilon_0+\varepsilon_1\le\alpha\). Conditional on \(\mathcal T\), a fixed feasible common \(w\), iid arrays, the independent common target \(T\), and selector auxiliary draws imply, for each arm, \[\Pr\!\left(S_{0,a}>r_{a,(k_a)}\mid\mathcal T\right)\le\frac{M+1-k_a}{M+1}=\rho_{M,\varepsilon_a}\le\varepsilon_a.\] Clipping to \([-K,K]\) and Bonferroni therefore give \[\Pr\!\left(\tau_T\in\mathcal C^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_w\mid\mathcal T\right)\ge1-\rho_{M,\varepsilon_0}-\rho_{M,\varepsilon_1}\ge1-\varepsilon_0-\varepsilon_1\ge1-\alpha,\] without arm independence. Under \(\varepsilon_0=\varepsilon_1=\alpha/2\), one has \(k_0=k_1=k\), \(I^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_{a,w}=I_{a,w}\), and \(\mathcal C^{\boldsymbol\varepsilon}_w=\mathcal C_w\), so the symmetric rank and coverage formulas follow as a corollary.
statements.justification: The convex-hull and Farkas characterization turns joint two-arm orbit balance with one common mixture into a finite, computable feasibility certificate; its asymmetric coverage conclusion uses the exact sum of the two armwise staircase errors.
statements.gap: Separate armwise mixtures would not implement the declared common selector design, and a symmetric-only conclusion hides unequal rank allocations.
statements.consumer: The protocol either obtains a feasible common mixture or a separating hyperplane explaining why none exists.

statements.statement: Assume that \(\mathcal H\) is finite and nonempty, and let \(w\in\Delta(\mathcal H)\) be fixed or prespecified. Let \(\epsilon_0,\epsilon_1>0\) be prespecified and satisfy \(\epsilon_0+\epsilon_1\le\alpha\), and let arm \(a\)'s weighted threshold in \(\texttt{def:weighted-rank-inversion}\) use its left-continuous \((1-\epsilon_a)\)-quantile. Conditional on \(\mathcal T\), orbit overlap implies \[\Pr_P(\tau_T\in\mathcal C^{\mathrm W}_w\mid\mathcal T)\ge1-\epsilon_0-\epsilon_1\ge1-\alpha\] for every \(P\in\mathcal P_{\mathcal G,K}\) and arbitrary fixed measurable trained predictor maps. Invariance makes score laws coincide within an orbit, so the orbit likelihood ratios suffice even with member-level selector singularity. Under joint balance, every relevant \(\lambda_{a,O}=1\), and \(r^{\mathrm W}_{a,w}=r_{a,(k_a)}\) for \(k_a=\lceil(M+1)(1-\epsilon_a)\rceil\), including the \(+\infty\) clause when \(k_a=M+1\); hence weighted inversion recovers ordinary arm-specific rank inversion. Taking \(\epsilon_0=\epsilon_1=\alpha/2\) gives \(k_0=k_1=\lceil(M+1)(1-\alpha/2)\rceil\) and recovers the original symmetric statement and formulas. The one-arm rank inequality is the finite-support likelihood-ratio weighted-exchangeability validity argument; the design-specific deductions are the orbit-quotient likelihood-ratio reduction, the sufficiency of quotient overlap under member-level singularity, and its integration with one common target, one common two-arm selector mixture, clipping, and Bonferroni.
statements.justification: The general weighted-exchangeability quantile theorem with test mass at \(+\infty\) is inherited from Tibshirani, Barber, Candès, and Ramdas \cite{TibshiraniBarberCandesRamdas2019}. The design-specific step is reduction to finite orbit likelihood ratios, sufficiency of quotient overlap under member-level singularity, and combination of two armwise guarantees for one common causal target by clipping and Bonferroni. Lei--Candès, Wang--Li--Yu, and Barber et al. delimit adjacent target, design, and nonexchangeability scopes \cite{LeiCandes2021,WangLiYu2025,BarberCandesRamdasTibshirani2023} without a priority claim.
statements.gap: The theorem does not introduce a new generic weighted-conformal principle and does not remove quotient overlap. Its contribution is the matched-randomization orbit reduction and common-target two-arm integration under normalized realized-arm selector kernels.
statements.consumer: Use orbit weights when exact selector balance fails but every target-supported orbit has calibration mass; combine the two prespecified arm levels only through clipping and Bonferroni.

statements.statement: If \(k=M+1\), then \(r_{0,(k)}=r_{1,(k)}=+\infty\), \(I_{0,w}=I_{1,w}=[-K,K]\), \(\mathcal C_w=[-2K,2K]\), and \(\Pr_P(\tau_T\in\mathcal C_w\mid\mathcal T)=1\) for every law and trained predictor maps.
statements.justification: The symmetric coverage-one boundary is the both-arm branch of a complete asymmetric case split.
statements.gap: The equal split cannot display a one-arm boundary.
statements.consumer: A report can distinguish one infinite threshold from two.

statements.statement: Suppose \(k\le M\), equivalently \(\alpha\ge2/(M+1)\), and, for one arm \(a\), every target-supported orbit has zero \(q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{w,a}\) mass. Then \(\texttt{def:disjoint-orbit-law}\) belongs to \(\mathcal P_{\mathcal G,K}\), all adverse-arm calibration scores equal \(0\), and \(r_{a,(k)}=0\). The other arm's scores and threshold equal \(0\), so \(\mathcal C_w=\{0\}\). The common target effect equals \(K\) for \(a=1\) and \(-K\) for \(a=0\), hence \(\Pr_P(\tau_T\in\mathcal C_w\mid\mathcal T)=0\).
statements.justification: The zero-coverage construction is formulated on actual \(\mathcal G\)-orbits and remains valid without any pair assumption.
statements.gap: A pair cell instantiates this witness only after its orbit status is established from the group action; the pure local-swap check is one sufficient route.
statements.consumer: Compute actual target and calibration orbit support and report whether \(k\le M\) before invoking zero coverage.

statements.statement: Fix a pair block \(b\) with \(\mathcal V_b=\{v_{b,0},v_{b,1}\}\) and suppose the local swap subgroup satisfies \(\langle s_b\rangle\le\mathcal G\). Then \(s_b(v_{b,r},a)=(v_{b,1-r},a)\) for \(r,a\in\{0,1\}\), with \(s_b\) fixing all outside members and relabelling complete records as in \(\texttt{def:group-action}\), and the global \(\mathcal G\)-orbits in block \(b\) are \(O_{b,0}=\{(v_{b,0},0),(v_{b,1},0)\}\) and \(O_{b,1}=\{(v_{b,0},1),(v_{b,1},1)\}\). If the target arm-\(1\) lifted law is supported on \(O_{b,1}\), an exclusive mate-control analogue supported on the realized mate's control state in \(O_{b,0}\) has a different orbit-mass vector, cannot satisfy exact joint balance, and is not a legal arm-\(1\) selector. Separately, if a legal implemented arm-\(1\) selector satisfies \(q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{w,1}(O_{b,1})=0\), then arm \(1\) satisfies the disjoint-orbit premise. Its zero-coverage conclusion applies when \(k\le M\); for \(k=M+1\), coverage is one by \(\texttt{prop:rank-boundary}\).
statements.justification: The two-member-block and pure local-swap hypotheses are a conservative sufficient certificate that both arm-specific pair cells are global orbits.
statements.gap: The certificate is not necessary, since a coupled-swap subgroup may generate the same orbit; mate-control selection remains illegal for the opposite arm.
statements.consumer: Verify the displayed sufficient audit, then check the orbit row, selector legality, and finite-rank boundary.

statements.statement: Let \(\mathcal V=\{v_{1,0},v_{1,1},v_{2,0},v_{2,1}\}\) and \(\mathcal G=S_2\times S_2\), with the generators in \(\texttt{def:pair-action}\). The four arm-specific orbits are \(O_{1,0},O_{1,1},O_{2,0},O_{2,1}\). If \(q_{\mathrm{tar}}\) concentrates on \(v_{1,0}\), its arm \(1\) orbit vector has mass \(1\) on \(O_{1,1}\). An exclusive mate-control analogue concentrated on \((v_{1,1},0)\) has mass \(1\) on \(O_{1,0}\), so the relevant stacked LP equations cannot both hold and the analogue is not a legal arm \(1\) selector. If a legal implemented selector has \(q^{\mathrm{obs}}_{w,1}(O_{1,1})=0\), then for \(k\le M\) the bounded witness has \(\mathcal C_w=\{0\}\) and coverage \(0\); for \(k=M+1\), it has \(I_{0,w}=I_{1,w}=[-K,K]\), \(\mathcal C_w=[-2K,2K]\), and coverage \(1\).
statements.justification: The declared \(S_2\times S_2\) product contains both required pure local swaps, so the sufficient audit certifies all four cells as global orbits.
statements.gap: This finite regression test does not cover an arbitrary four-member group; such a group requires its own orbit computation.
statements.consumer: Use the four certified orbit rows to test selector-LP construction and both symmetric rank regimes.

sampling_model.design: Known matched-block randomization \(\nu\) is invariant under the declared within-block member action; particular pair examples specialize this design by imposing exactly one treated member per pair.
sampling_model.units: The analysis uses iid replicated latent matched-block arrays observed as \(\mathsf O_i\). The Shamba Maisha consumer has eight matched pairs, but none of these results is design-based coverage for its single fixed finite population.
sampling_model.treatment: Potential-outcome arm labels are preserved by \(g\cdot(v,a)=(\pi_g(v),a)\). A single target \(T\) is independent of training, every latent array, every assignment, and all selector auxiliary randomization; it is lifted coherently to \(T^0\) and \(T^1\).
sampling_model.overlap: Observable kernels choose only an observed realized-arm member. The unweighted LP is \(Aw=b_{\mathrm{LP}}\) for one \(w\); when it fails, weighted rank inversion uses \(p_{a,O}>0\Rightarrow q_{a,O}>0\).
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: Each \(\widehat m_a=\widehat m_a(\mathcal T)\) is trained only on independent external observed training data. Scores, LP inputs, weights, thresholds, and reported intervals use only observed records, known \(\nu\), prespecified target information, and auxiliary randomization.

estimand_functional: \(\mathcal C^{\mathrm W}_w=I^{\mathrm W}_{1,w}-I^{\mathrm W}_{0,w},\quad\Pr_P(\tau_T\in\mathcal C^{\mathrm W}_w\mid\mathcal T)\ge1-\alpha\)

bibliography.citation: Bingkai Wang, Fan Li, and Mengxin Yu (2025), "Conformal causal inference for cluster randomized trials: model-robust inference without asymptotic approximations," Statistica Sinica preprint SS-2025-0476, https://www3.stat.sinica.edu.tw/statistica/fp/SS-2025-0476.html.
Jesse Hemerik and Jelle Goeman (2018), "Exact testing with random permutations," TEST 27, 811--825.
Aaditya Ramdas, Rina Foygel Barber, Emmanuel Candès, and Ryan J. Tibshirani (2023), "Permutation tests using arbitrary permutation distributions," Sankhya A.

Ryan J. Tibshirani, Rina Foygel Barber, Emmanuel J. Candès, and Aaditya Ramdas (2019), ``Conformal Prediction Under Covariate Shift,'' Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32, https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2019/hash/8fb21ee7a2207526da55a679f0332de2-Abstract.html.

Lihua Lei and Emmanuel J. Candès (2021), ``Conformal Inference of Counterfactuals and Individual Treatment Effects,'' Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B 83(5), 911--938, https://doi.org/10.1111/rssb.12445.

Rina Foygel Barber, Emmanuel J. Candès, Aaditya Ramdas, and Ryan J. Tibshirani (2023), ``Conformal Prediction Beyond Exchangeability,'' Annals of Statistics 51(2), 816--845, https://doi.org/10.1214/23-AOS2276.

Edgar Dobriban and Mengxin Yu (2025), "SymmPI: predictive inference for data with group symmetries," Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B: Statistical Methodology 87(5), 1353--1381, https://doi.org/10.1093/jrsssb/qkaf022.
Yuehao Bai, Jizhou Liu, Azeem M. Shaikh, and Max Tabord-Meehan (2024), "Inference in cluster randomized trials with matched pairs," Journal of Econometrics 245, 105873.
Craig R. Cohen et al. (2022), "Shamba Maisha: cluster randomized controlled trial of a multisectoral agricultural intervention to improve HIV health outcomes in Kenya," JAMA Network Open 5, e2246158.

Chao Gao, Liren Shan, Vaidehi Srinivas, and Aravindan Vijayaraghavan (2025), ``Volume Optimality in Conformal Prediction with Structured Prediction Sets,'' Proceedings of the 42nd International Conference on Machine Learning, Proceedings of Machine Learning Research 267, 18495--18527, https://proceedings.mlr.press/v267/gao25k.html.

Regis Lebrun (2013), ``Efficient time/space algorithm to compute rectangular probabilities of multinomial, multivariate hypergeometric and multivariate Polya distributions,'' Statistics and Computing 23(5), 615--623, https://doi.org/10.1007/s11222-012-9334-8.

Alexander Timans, Christoph-Nikolas Straehle, Kaspar Sakmann, Christian A. Naesseth, and Eric Nalisnick (2025), ``Max-Rank: Efficient Multiple Testing for Conformal Prediction,'' Proceedings of the 28th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics, Proceedings of Machine Learning Research 258, 3898--3906, https://proceedings.mlr.press/v258/timans25a.html.

Filip Schlembach, Evgueni Smirnov, and Mark H. M. Winands (2025), ``Dynamic Conformal Prediction for Multi-Target Regression: Optimising Informational Efficiency under Joint Validity,'' Proceedings of Machine Learning Research 266, 193--213, https://proceedings.mlr.press/v266/schlembach25a.html.

tldr: For iid replicated matched-block experiments with a known invariant assignment law, equality of calibration and target mass on every member orbit is necessary and sufficient for universal armwise score-law equality. Observable selectors are normalized and supported on the requested realized arm for assignments with positive design mass, with auxiliary draws conditional on assignment; assignments outside that support receive zero integrated weight. A finite stacked linear program finds a common two-arm selector under exact balance, quotient likelihood-ratio weighting gives armwise conformal validity under orbit overlap, and ordinary ranks admit exact-balance, total-variation, disjoint-support, and matched-pair certificates. Separately, a preorder and memoized four-cell algorithm exactly evaluates \(\Gamma_{M,\alpha,\boldsymbol\varepsilon}(w)\) only for the deterministic bounded orbit-constant-score audit submodel. It retains one coherent target and within-replicate cross-arm dependence but is not a full-model joint-inclusion or causal effect-set coverage theorem.

project_justification.gap: Finite-group testing and group-symmetry prediction establish validity from invariance, while causal and cluster-randomized conformal work studies counterfactual or treatment-effect targets under its own assignment regimes. Those scopes do not themselves decide whether an assignment-based calibration selector and a deployment target induce the same score law on a declared finite orbit quotient. The computational literature treats general discrete rectangular probabilities, simultaneous conformal error control, multi-target error allocation, or structured-set volume, all of which differ from this paper's restricted deterministic orbit-score audit functional.
project_justification.niche: The note separates four layers for iid replicated matched-block experiments: universal calibration/deployment score-law equality on a finite orbit quotient; common-selector feasibility; quotient-weighted and ordinary-rank causal guarantees; and exact computation of a deliberately restricted deterministic joint rank-deficit audit. The selector conditions are normalization, realized-arm support on the positive-design support, and conditional auxiliary draws; assignments outside that support receive zero integrated weight.
project_justification.fill: The orbit-mass equivalence supplies a necessity witness as well as sufficiency, the stacked linear program supplies a computable common selector, quotient likelihood ratios supply weighted armwise validity despite member-level singularity, and clipping plus Bonferroni produces one common-member effect guarantee. The asymmetric preorder evaluator preserves the coherent common target and within-replicate cross-arm dependence and reports exact time and memory charges for the paper-defined deterministic audit functional, while explicitly excluding stochastic conditional residual scores from that frontier.

related_work: Hemerik and Goeman and Ramdas, Barber, Candès, and Tibshirani study finite permutation or group-testing reference mechanisms \cite{HemerikGoeman2018,RamdasBarberCandesTibshirani2023}; Dobriban and Mengxin Yu study predictive inference under group symmetry \cite{DobribanYu2025}. The orbit-balance theorem instead concerns iid replicated invariant latent arrays, assignment-based calibration selection, equality of calibration and deployment score laws, and a universal finite-orbit mass equivalence with an orbit-separating necessity witness. Lei and Candès and Wang, Li, and Yu are causal and cluster-randomized conformal comparators for target and design scope \cite{LeiCandes2021,WangLiYu2025}. Tibshirani, Barber, Candès, and Ramdas supply the inherited likelihood-ratio weighted-exchangeability theorem with the test-point mass at \(+\infty\) \cite{TibshiraniBarberCandesRamdas2019}; this manuscript's weighted design step is the finite orbit quotient, quotient overlap despite member-level singularity, and two armwise guarantees combined for one common causal target by clipping and Bonferroni. For computation, Lebrun treats arbitrary rectangles for several discrete count laws \cite{Lebrun2013}; Timans et al. study dependence-aware simultaneous conformal family-wise error control \cite{TimansEtAl2025}; Schlembach, Smirnov, and Winands optimize multi-target hyperrectangle volume under joint coverage \cite{SchlembachSmirnovWinands2025}; and Gao et al. optimize structured prediction-set volume \cite{GaoShanSrinivasVijayaraghavan2025}. This manuscript instead exactly evaluates the explicitly restricted deterministic bounded orbit-constant joint rank-deficit functional with asymmetric ranks, one coherent target, and within-replicate cross-arm dependence. The comparison asserts neither priority nor a general coverage theorem.
interpretation: For the asymmetric ordinary procedure, simultaneous inclusion of \(Y_{0T}(1)\) and \(Y_{0T}(0)\) in their arm intervals implies inclusion of \(\tau_T\) in their Minkowski difference, but the converse can fail. Exact orbit balance makes each arm's calibration and target scores iid because their orbit mixtures coincide. The observable selector conditions used for this result are normalization and requested realized-arm support on \(\operatorname{supp}(\nu)\), together with conditional auxiliary draws; assignments outside the support have zero integrated weight. Quotient weighting corrects unequal orbit masses under quotient overlap. The deterministic frontier answers a different audit question: it computes the worst joint rank-inclusion deficit over deterministic orbit-score maps, preserving the common target and cross-arm calibration coupling. A full-model residual may remain random inside an orbit, so the audit cannot be read as causal effect-set coverage for every invariant law.
technical_internal_limitation: All probability guarantees use iid replicated latent arrays, independent external training, a prespecified common target, a known invariant assignment law, normalized observable selector kernels supported on the requested realized arm on the assignment support, and independent selector auxiliary draws conditional on assignment. Off-support kernel extensions are immaterial because they receive zero design weight. The weighted theorem additionally requires orbit overlap. The exact frontier is restricted to deterministic bounded orbit-constant score maps and can be combinatorial in the arm-orbit counts; it is neither a supremum over all invariant latent laws nor a uniform full-model joint-inclusion or effect-set coverage certificate.
honest_scope: The manuscript proves finite-sample results for the stated replicated matched-block model, not design-based coverage for one fixed Shamba Maisha finite population. Exact balance gives ordinary split-conformal validity; imbalance gives active-arm total-variation bounds and explicit failures; quotient overlap gives weighted validity. The \(S_2\) results require audited pair orbits. Selector kernels need only be normalized and supported on the requested realized arm for positive-mass assignments, together with the declared conditional selector draws; off-support extensions are immaterial. The quantities \(\Gamma_{M,\alpha,\boldsymbol\varepsilon}(w)\) and \(\Gamma_{M,\alpha}(w)\) are exact only for deterministic bounded orbit-constant scores and are presented as audit evaluators, not as new general coverage theorems.
